content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Michael Bradbury 
2367 Marina Glen Way 
Sacramento, CA 95833 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. 1-19-037 
Dear Mr. Bradbury: 
March 25, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the post-government employment 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Because your question is general in nature, we 
are treating your request as one for informal assistance.2 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest-prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (197 5) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
After retiring in December 2018 as the Permit Program Manager for the Department of 
Water Resources ("DWR"), may you continue your work (acquiring permits for a specific statewide 
project) as a sub-contractor to a recently formed joint powers authority, given that the joint powers 
authority is now responsible for acquiring the project permits? 
CONCLUSION 
Under the Act's "revolving door" provisions, you are permanently prohibited from working 
to acquire permits you previously worked on as the DWR Program Manager if the matter is before 
an administrative agency of the State of California. However, you may participate in (1) new 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation l 8329(c)(3).) 


File No. 1-19-037 
Page No. 2 
proceedings, (2) proceedings you did not previously participate in, and (3) proceedings you did 
participate in, but are before a non-state administrative agency. Additionally, under the Act's "one-
year ban," you are prohibited from appearing before or communicating with DWR for the purpose 
of influencing any administrative decision. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
You previously worked for the Department of Water Resources ("DWR") as the Permit 
Program Manager for the WaterFix Project ("the Project"), which is a designated position under 
DWR's conflict of interest code. As the Permit Program Manager, you were responsible for 
acquiring various Project permits, including "the 404 wetlands permit, 106 cultural resources 
compliance, and 1602 streambed alteration agreement." You led teams comprised of staff from 
DWR and other agencies, as well as consultants, in the development of applications and supporting 
documentation necessary to acquire permits from regulatory agencies. You had no part in 
developing or awarding contracts to any consultants involved in the Project, and you had no 
communications with any consultants regarding employment while you were still working for 
DWR. You retired from DWR in December 2018. 
DWR has entered into an agreement with the Delta Conveyance Design and Construction 
Authority ("DCA"), an independent joint powers authority consisting of local government entities, 
and DWR has transferred to DCA the responsibility to acquire certain permits. After acquiring 
those permits, DCA may also be tasked with amending the permits and ensuring they are complied 
with. It is unclear ifDWR will remain the official applicant for each permit, or whether DCA will 
become the official applicant for at least some of the permits it is responsible for acquiring. DWR 
will have oversight of the permits otherwise handled by DCA, such as ensuring state engineering 
standards and regulatory permits are complied with. 
DCA is currently contracting with consultants to complete the permits it is now responsible 
for. After you retired from DWR, several consulting firms asked you to join them on proposals for 
contracts with DCA. Accordingly, you are considering working as a sub-contractor for a consulting 
firm that contracts with DCA for the Project. You believe that your work would initially involve 
acquiring various permits for the Project, after which your work might involve having the permits 
amended and ensuring compliance with the permits. At least some of the permits you would be 
working to acquire are permits you had begun working to acquire while employed by DWR, when 
procurement of those permits was still the responsibility of DWR. 
ANALYSIS 
Public officials who have left state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act, colloquially known as the "revolving door" prohibitions. The 
first is the "permanent ban" provision, which prohibits a former state employee from "representing 
any other person ( other than the State of California) and participating, for compensation, in any 
proceeding involving specific parties before a court or state administrative agency if the proceeding 
is one in which the former state employee participated while employed by the state. (See Sections 
87401-87402, Regulation 18741.1.) The second is the "one-year ban" provision, which prohibits 
certain state employees from communicating, for compensation, with their former agency for the 


File No. 1-19-037 
Page No. 3 
purpose of influencing certain administrative or legislative action. (Section 87406, Regulation 
18746.1.) 
The Permanent Ban 
Under Section 87401, after retiring, no former state administrative official may act as a 
compensated agent, or otherwise represent, any other person ( other than the State of California) 
before any court or state administrative agency or any officer or employee thereof by making any 
formal or informal appearance, or by making any oral or written communication with the intent to 
influence, in connection with any judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding if both of the 
following apply: 
(a) 
The State of California is a party or has a direct and substantial interest; and 
(b) 
The proceeding is one in which the former state administrative official participated. 
The former state administrative official is also prohibited from being compensated for 
aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in representing any other person ( except the 
State of California) in any proceeding in which he would be prohibited from himself appearing 
under Section 87401. (Section 87402.) 
A 'judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" means "any proceeding, application, request 
for a ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, 
arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state 
administrative agency .... " (Section 87400(c).) An official or employee has "participated" in a 
proceeding ifhe or she took part in the proceeding "personally, and substantially" through 
"decision, approval, disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial 
basis, investigation, or use of confidential information .... " (Section 87400( d).) 
The permanent ban does not apply to "new" proceedings even in cases where the new 
proceeding is related to, or grows out of, a prior proceeding in which the official had participated. 
For example, we have previously advised that the implementation of a contract is considered a 
"new" proceeding in relation to the awarding of that contract. (Yam Advice Letter, No. 1-17-292; 
Starovoytov Advice Letter, No. A-14-149). However, where an employee has already participated 
in the implementation of a contract, he or she may not work for compensation for an outside 
employer on the same implementation proceeding. (Yam Advice Letter, No. 1-17-292; Culp Advice 
Letter, No. 1-14-051.) 
As a former designated employee ofDWR, you are subject to the Act's permanent ban. As 
the Permit Program Manager for DWR, your work involved the development of applications and 
supporting documentation necessary to acquire permits from regulatory agencies. Thus, you 
personally and substantially participated in quasi-judicial proceedings, i.e., permit applications. 
Further, responsibility for those proceedings has been transferred to DCA. As an employee of a 
private sub-contractor for DCA, you would be working on behalf of a person other than the State of 
California to acquire the same permits you were working to acquire while employed by DWR. 
Accordingly, the proceedings before the permit-issuing agencies are not "new," and the permanent 
ban is potentially applicable. As such, you may not act as an agent for or otherwise represent a 


File No. I-19-037 
Page No. 4 
consulting firm or DCA (a non-state agency) in any of those proceedings if the proceeding is before 
a court or state administrative agency, nor may you aid, advise, counsel, consult, or assist anyone 
else in doing so. (Sections 87401, 87402.) Conversely, acting as a sub-contractor for DCA and 
working to acquire permits fromfederal or local agencies would not violate the permanent ban if 
the work does not involve proceedings before a court or state administrative agency. Depending on 
how you conduct your work, however, you may still be at risk of violating the one-year ban, as 
discussed below. 
The One-Year Ban 
The "one-year ban" prohibits a former designated employee from making, for 
compensation, any formal or informal appearance, or making any oral or written communication, 
before his or her former agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative 
actions or any discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a 
permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (See Section 87406; 
Regulation 18746.1.) 
The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who holds a 
position that is designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code. (Section 
87406(d)(l); Regulation 18746.l(a)(2).) The ban applies for twelve months from the date the 
employee permanently leaves state office or employment. While in effect, the one-year ban applies 
only when a former employee or official is being compensated for his or her appearances or 
communications before his or her former agency on behalf of any person as an agent, attorney, or 
representative of that person. (Regulation 187 46.1 (b )(3) and ( 4 ). ) 
In contrast to the permanent ban, which only applies to certain matters involving specific 
parties such as 'judicial or quasi-judicial" proceedings, the one-year ban applies to "any appearance 
or communication made for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action or 
influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation 
of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." (Regulation 
18746. l(b)(S).) An appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" if it is made 
for the "principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or 
advancing the action or proceeding." (Regulation 18746.2.) An appearance or communication 
includes, but is not limited to, conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by 
electronic transmission, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication. (Ibid) 
Finally, appearances and communications are prohibited only if they are (1) before a state 
agency that the public official worked for or represented, (2) before a state agency "whose budget, 
personnel, and other operations" are subject to the control of a state agency the public official 
worked for or represented, or (3) before any state agency subject to the direction and control of the 
Governor, if the official was a designated employee of the Governor's office during the twelve 
months before leaving state office or employment. (Regulation 18746.l(b)(6).) 
You have noted that although DWR has transferred responsibility for acquiring certain 
Project permits to DCA, DWR retains oversight over the permitting process. To the extent that the 
acquisition of any Project permit may now require review and approval by DWR, in addition to the 
agency responsible for actually issuing the respective permit, DWR's oversight would constitute an 


File No. 1-19-037 
Page No. 5 
"action involving the issuance of a permit." Under the Act, you are prohibited from appearing 
before or communicating with DWR for the purpose of influencing that action, as described above. 
Specifically, your name must not appear in any documents that are presented to DWR and you must 
abstain from any direct contact with your former employer. This prohibition applies regardless of 
whether the proceeding is "new" or a continuation of your previous work for DWR. 
In sum, the permanent ban prohibits you from acquiring, or even helping to acquire, any of 
the permits you previously sought to acquire when you worked for DWR if the permit application 
proceeding is before a court or state agency. If the permit proceeding is new or you did not 
participate in the proceeding while you worked for DWR, or if the proceeding is before a non-state 
agency, the permanent ban does not prohibit you from working to acquire the permit. In any case, 
however, the one-year ban will prohibit you from appearing before or communicating with DWR 
with respect to any administrative decisions DWR may be required to make. Thus, to the extent that 
your work is not prohibited by the permanent ban, the one-year ban will require (among other 
things) that your name not appear on any of the documents subject to review and approval by 
DWR. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
KMC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Kevin Cornwall 
Counsel, Legal Division

authority is now responsible for acquiring the project permits?

first is the "permanent ban" provision, which prohibits a former state employee from "representing 
any other person ( other than the State of California) and participating, for compensation, in any

certain state employees from communicating, for compensation, with their former agency for the  
purpose of influencing certain administrative or legislative action. (Section 87406, Regulation 
18746.1.)

compensated agent, or otherwise represent, any other person ( other than the State of California)

influence, in connection with any judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding if both of the

(a) 
The State of California is a party or has a direct and substantial interest; and 
(b) 
The proceeding is one in which the former state administrative official participated.

under Section 87401. (Section 87402.)

The "one-year ban" prohibits a former designated employee from making, for 
compensation, any formal or informal appearance, or making any oral or written communication,

Water Resources ("DWR"), may you continue your work (acquiring permits for a specific statewide 
project) as a sub-contractor to a recently formed joint powers authority, given that the joint powers 
authority is now responsible for acquiring the project permits?

written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation l 8329(c)(3).)  
proceedings, (2) proceedings you did not previously participate in, and (3) proceedings you did 
participate in, but are before a non-state administrative agency. Additionally, under the Act's "one-
year ban," you are prohibited from appearing before or communicating with DWR for the purpose 
of influencing any administrative decision.
